content:
# Leviticus Chapter 23 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 71v–72r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_023_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_023_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 109–110 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_023_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_023_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 23 is the great sacred-calendar chapter of the Holiness Code — one of three comprehensive festival-calendars in the Torah (alongside Exod 23:14–17 and Deut 16:1–17), and by far the most detailed. Its governing term is mo'adei YHWH — "the appointed times of the LORD" (vv.2, 4, 37, 44) — a noun built on the root y-'-d, "to appoint/meet," pointing to times when the LORD and his people convene. The chapter falls into seven major sections corresponding to seven feast-occasions: (1) the weekly Sabbath — shabbat shabbaton — set as the structural foundation of all appointed times (v.3); (2) Passover (pesach) on 14th Nisan and the seven-day Feast of Unleavened Bread (chag ha-matsot) beginning 15th Nisan, with opening and closing miqra-qodesh ("holy convocations") on days 1 and 7 and a prohibition of melaket avodah ("laborious work") on those days (vv.4–8); (3) the Firstfruits/omer law — the sheaf (omer) of first-grain waved on the day after the sabbath (mi-macharat ha-shabbat) beginning the count to Shavuot (vv.9–14); (4) the Feast of Weeks (Shavuot/Pentecost) — fifty days after the omer, with a new grain offering, two loaves of leavened bread (lechem chamets) as bikkurim, and accompanying sacrifices (vv.15–22); (5) the Day of Trumpet Blast (yom teru'ah) on the 1st of the 7th month — a day of rest and holy convocation (vv.23–25); (6) the Day of Atonements (Yom Kippurim) on the 10th of the 7th month — inui nefesh ("self-affliction"), complete cessation of labor, and the karet-threat (vv.26–32); and (7) the Feast of Booths (Sukkot) from the 15th through the 22nd of the 7th month, with the unique command ba-sukkot teshvu — "you shall dwell in booths" — as a memorial of the wilderness sojourn (vv.33–43). Verse 44 is the closing summary formula. Throughout the chapter, four recurring structural markers appear: (a) "a holy convocation" (miqra-qodesh); (b) a prohibition of laborious work (melaket avodah); (c) fire-offerings to the LORD (isheh la-YHWH); and (d) the self-identification formula "I am the LORD your God." The chapter's theological logic is that sacred time — like sacred space (Lev 17–22) and sacred persons (Lev 8–10, 21) — requires the same pattern of consecration, separation from ordinary labor, and approach to the divine presence.]*

---

## Leviticus 23

**23:1** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**23:2** Speak to the sons of Yisra'el and say to them: The appointed times of the LORD [mo'adei YHWH — מוֹעֲדֵי יְהוָה; mo'adim — singular mo'ed, from y-'-d, "to appoint/meet"; the fixed times when the LORD convenes with his people] which you shall proclaim [asher-tiqre'u otam — אֲשֶׁר-תִּקְרְאוּ אֹתָם; the verb q-r-' here means "to proclaim/call out," the official declaration that constitutes the convocation] as holy convocations [miqra'ei qodesh — מִקְרָאֵי קֹדֶשׁ; miqra — "a calling/convocation," from the same root q-r-'; thus "callings of holiness" or "holy summonings"] — these are my appointed times [elleh hem mo'adai — אֵלֶּה הֵם מוֹעֲדָי].

**23:3** Six days [sheshet yamim — שֵׁשֶׁת יָמִים] shall labor be done [te'aseh melakhah — תֵּעָשֶׂה מְלָאכָה; the passive "labor shall be done" rather than "you shall labor" — the formulation is impersonal], and on the seventh day is a sabbath of complete rest [ve-va-yom ha-shevi'i shabbat shabbaton — וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי שַׁבַּת שַׁבָּתוֹן; shabbaton — an intensive or abstract noun from shabbat; "a sabbath-rest" or "a rest of resting/cessation"], a holy convocation [miqra-qodesh — מִקְרָא-קֹדֶשׁ]; all labor you shall not do [kol-melakhah lo ta'asu — כָּל-מְלָאכָה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ]; it is a sabbath to the LORD [shabbat hi la-YHWH — שַׁבָּת הִוא לַיהוָה] in all your dwellings [be-khol moshvoteikhem — בְּכֹל מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם].

**23:4** These are the appointed times of the LORD [elleh mo'adei YHWH — אֵלֶּה מוֹעֲדֵי יְהוָה], holy convocations [miqra'ei qodesh — מִקְרָאֵי קֹדֶשׁ] which you shall proclaim [asher-tiqre'u otam — אֲשֶׁר-תִּקְרְאוּ אֹתָם] at their appointed times [be-mo'adam — בְּמוֹעֲדָם].

**23:5** In the first month [ba-chodesh ha-rishon — בַּחֹדֶשׁ הָרִאשׁוֹן; Nisan, the month of the Exodus], on the fourteenth of the month [be-arba'ah asar la-chodesh — בְּאַרְבָּעָה עָשָׂר לַחֹדֶשׁ] between the two evenings [bein ha-arbayim — בֵּין הָעַרְבָּיִם; lit. "between the two evenings" — the transitional period from late afternoon until full dark; the time of the Passover slaughter], is the Passover [pesach — פֶּסַח; from p-s-ch, "to pass over/to skip over"; the name of both the sacrifice and the festival] to the LORD.

**23:6** And on the fifteenth day of this month [u-ve-chamishah asar yom la-chodesh ha-zeh — וּבַחֲמִשָּׁה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ הַזֶּה] is the Feast of Unleavened Bread [chag ha-matsot — חַג הַמַּצּוֹת] to the LORD [la-YHWH — לַיהוָה]; seven days [shivat yamim — שִׁבְעַת יָמִים] you shall eat unleavened bread [matsot tokhelu — מַצּוֹת תֹּאכֵלוּ].

**23:7** On the first day [ba-yom ha-rishon — בַּיּוֹם הָרִאשׁוֹן] you shall have a holy convocation [miqra-qodesh yihyeh lakhem — מִקְרָא-קֹדֶשׁ יִהְיֶה לָכֶם]; all laborious work [kol-melechet avodah — כָּל-מְלֶאכֶת עֲבֹדָה; melechet avodah — a distinctive compound: "labor-work" or "work of toil/servitude," a more intensive prohibition than ordinary melakhah] you shall not do.

**23:8** But you shall present a fire-offering to the LORD [ve-hiqravtem isheh la-YHWH — וְהִקְרַבְתֶּם אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה] for seven days [shivat yamim — שִׁבְעַת יָמִים]; on the seventh day is a holy convocation [ba-yom ha-shevi'i miqra-qodesh — בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי מִקְרָא-קֹדֶשׁ]; all laborious work you shall not do [kol-melechet avodah lo ta'asu — כָּל-מְלֶאכֶת עֲבֹדָה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ].

**23:9** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**23:10** Speak to the sons of Yisra'el and say to them: When you come into the land [ki-tavo'u el-ha-arets — כִּי-תָבֹאוּ אֶל-הָאָרֶץ] which I am giving to you [asher ani noten lakhem — אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי נֹתֵן לָכֶם] and you harvest its harvest [u-qtsartem et-qetsira — וּקְצַרְתֶּם אֶת-קְצִירָהּ], you shall bring [va-haveilem — וַהֲבֵאתֶם] the first sheaf [et-omer reshit qetsirkhem — אֶת-עֹמֶר רֵאשִׁית קְצִירְכֶם; omer — the unit of measure (approximately two liters) and here the bundle/sheaf; reshit — "first/beginning," the same root as bereshit] of your harvest to the priest [el-ha-kohen — אֶל-הַכֹּהֵן].

**23:11** And he shall wave [ve-henif — וְהֵנִיף; the Hifil of n-w-f, "to wave/move back and forth"; the tenufah — wave offering — is a distinctive ritual gesture] the sheaf [et-ha-omer — אֶת-הָעֹמֶר] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH — לִפְנֵי יְהוָה] for your acceptance [li-rtsone'khem — לִרְצֹנְכֶם]; on the day after the sabbath [mi-macharat ha-shabbat — מִמָּחֳרַת הַשַּׁבָּת; the verse at the center of the great Shavuot-date controversy: what "sabbath" is meant? The Sabbath of Passover week? The first day of Passover? See notes.] the priest shall wave it [yenifenu ha-kohen — יְנִיפֶנּוּ הַכֹּהֵן].

**23:12** And on the day you wave the sheaf [u-va-yom hanifkhem et-ha-omer — וּבְיוֹם הֲנִיפְכֶם אֶת-הָעֹמֶר] you shall prepare [ta'asu — תַּעֲשׂוּ; lit. "you shall do/make"] a male lamb [kesev tamim — כֶּבֶשׂ תָּמִים; a male lamb without blemish] without blemish [ben-shnato — בֶּן-שְׁנָתוֹ; lit. "a son of its year," i.e., in its first year], a year old [ben-shnato], as a burnt offering [le-olah — לְעֹלָה] to the LORD.

**23:13** And its grain offering [ve-minchatah — וּמִנְחָתָהּ; minchah — the cereal/grain offering accompanying the animal sacrifice] shall be two-tenths [shnei esronim — שְׁנֵי עֶשְׂרֹנִים; esron — a tenth of an ephah, approximately two liters] of fine flour [solet — סֹלֶת; solet — sifted wheat flour, the finest grade] mixed with oil [belulah ba-shemen — בְּלוּלָה בַשֶּׁמֶן], a fire-offering to the LORD [isheh la-YHWH — אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה], a pleasing aroma [reah nihoach — רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ; the standard formula of sacrifice-acceptability: "a soothing/pleasing aroma"]; and its drink offering [ve-niskah — וְנִסְכָּהּ; nesekh — "libation/drink offering," liquid poured out] shall be a quarter of a hin [reviat ha-hin — רְבִיעִת הַהִין; a hin is approximately 3.6 liters; a quarter hin is approximately 0.9 liters] of wine.

**23:14** And bread [ve-lechem — וְלֶחֶם; here meaning bread or any grain-product] and roasted grain [ve-qali — וְקָלִי; qali — parched/roasted grain] and fresh ears [ve-karmel — וְכַרְמֶל; karmel — fresh, full ears of grain, not yet dried; a rare term] you shall not eat [lo tokhelu — לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ] until this very day [ad-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh — עַד-עֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה; etsem ha-yom — "the bone/substance of this day," an emphatic idiom meaning "this very day"] until you bring [ad-haviakhem — עַד-הֲבִיאֲכֶם] the offering of your God [et-qorban Eloheikem — אֶת-קָרְבַּן אֱלֹהֵיכֶם]; it is a statute forever [chuqqat olam — חֻקַּת עוֹלָם] throughout your generations [le-doroteikem — לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם] in all your dwellings [be-khol moshvoteikhem — בְּכֹל מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם].

**23:15** And you shall count for yourselves [u-sefartem lakhem — וּסְפַרְתֶּם לָכֶם; the command of active personal counting — each household counts, not merely an official declaration] from the day after the sabbath [mi-macharat ha-shabbat — מִמָּחֳרַת הַשַּׁבָּת] from the day you bring [mi-yom havi'akhem — מִיּוֹם הֲבִיאֲכֶם] the omer of the wave offering [et-omer ha-tenufah — אֶת-עֹמֶר הַתְּנוּפָה] — seven complete weeks [sheva shabbatot temimot — שֶׁבַע שַׁבָּתוֹת תְּמִימֹת; shabbatot — "sabbaths/weeks"; temimot — "complete/whole," the same root as tamim; "complete weeks/full weeks"; the requirement of completeness ensures the count is a full seven cycles].

**23:16** Until the day after the seventh week [ad mi-macharat ha-shabbat ha-shevi'it — עַד מִמָּחֳרַת הַשַּׁבָּת הַשְּׁבִיעִת] you shall count fifty days [tisperu chamishim yom — תִּסְפְּרוּ חֲמִשִּׁים יוֹם; the fifty days give Shavuot its Greek name Pentēkostē — "fiftieth"], and you shall present a new grain offering [ve-hiqravtem minchah chadashah — וְהִקְרַבְתֶּם מִנְחָה חֲדָשָׁה] to the LORD.

**23:17** From your dwellings [mi-moshvoteikhem — מִמּוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם] you shall bring [tavi'u — תָּבִיאוּ] two loaves of bread [lechem tenufahshnayim — לֶחֶם תְּנוּפָה שְׁתַּיִם; lit. "bread of wave-offering, two"] as a wave offering [shnei esronim solet tihyenu — שְׁנֵי עֶשְׂרֹנִים סֹלֶת יִהְיֶינָה]; they shall be of two-tenths of fine flour [shnei esronim solet tihyenu — שְׁנֵי עֶשְׂרֹנִים סֹלֶת יִהְיֶינָה], they shall be baked leavened [chamets te'afenah — חָמֵץ תֵּאָפֶינָה; chamets — leavened/fermented; strikingly, this is the one instance in the festival-legislation where leavened bread is explicitly commanded for a sanctuary offering]; firstfruits to the LORD [bikkurim la-YHWH — בִּכּוּרִים לַיהוָה].

**23:18** And you shall present [ve-hiqravtem — וְהִקְרַבְתֶּם] along with the bread [al-ha-lechem — עַל-הַלֶּחֶם] seven male lambs [shivah kevasim — שִׁבְעָה כְבָשִׂים] without blemish [temimim — תְּמִימִם] sons of a year [benei shanah — בְּנֵי שָׁנָה] and one bull of the herd [ve-par echad ben-bakar — וּפַר אֶחָד בֶּן-בָּקָר] and two rams [u-shnei eilim — וּשְׁנֵי אֵילִם]; they shall be [yihyu — יִהְיוּ] a burnt offering [olah — עֹלָה] to the LORD, with their grain offering [u-minchatam — וּמִנְחָתָם] and their drink offerings [ve-niskeihem — וְנִסְכֵּיהֶם], a fire-offering [isheh — אִשֶּׁה] of pleasing aroma [reah nihoach — רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ] to the LORD.

**23:19** And you shall prepare [va-asitem — וַעֲשִׂיתֶם] one male goat [se'ir izzim echad — שְׂעִיר-עִזִּים אֶחָד] as a sin offering [le-chatat — לְחַטָּאת], and two male lambs [u-shnei kevasim — וּשְׁנֵי כְבָשִׂים] sons of a year [benei shanah — בְּנֵי שָׁנָה] as a peace offering [li-zevach shelamim — לִזְבַח שְׁלָמִים].

**23:20** And the priest shall wave them [ve-henif ha-kohen otam — וְהֵנִיף הַכֹּהֵן אֹתָם] along with the bread of the firstfruits [al-lechem ha-bikkurim — עַל-לֶחֶם הַבִּכּוּרִים] as a wave offering [tenufah — תְּנוּפָה] before the LORD [lifnei YHWH — לִפְנֵי יְהוָה], along with the two lambs [al-shnei ha-kevasim — עַל-שְׁנֵי הַכְּבָשִׂים]; they shall be holy [yihyu qodesh — יִהְיוּ קֹדֶשׁ] to the LORD [la-YHWH — לַיהוָה] for the priest [la-kohen — לַכֹּהֵן].

**23:21** And you shall proclaim on this very day [u-qratem be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh — וּקְרָאתֶם בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה] — a holy convocation it shall be for you [miqra-qodesh yihyeh lakhem — מִקְרָא-קֹדֶשׁ יִהְיֶה לָכֶם]; all laborious work you shall not do [kol-melechet avodah lo ta'asu — כָּל-מְלֶאכֶת עֲבֹדָה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ]; it is a statute forever [chuqqat olam — חֻקַּת עוֹלָם] in all your dwellings throughout your generations [be-khol moshvoteikhem le-doroteikem — בְּכֹל מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם].

**23:22** And when you harvest the harvest of your land [u-ve-qutzkhem et-qetsir artsekhem — וּבְקֻצְרְכֶם אֶת-קְצִיר אַרְצְכֶם] you shall not complete the corner of your field when you harvest [lo tekhaleh pe'at sadkha be-qutzkha — לֹא תְכַלֶּה פְּאַת שָׂדְךָ בְּקֻצְרֶךָ; pe'ah — the "corner/edge" of the field, the portion left unharvested for the poor; the root k-l-h means "to complete/finish/consume"], and the gleaning of your harvest [ve-leket qetsirekha — וְלֶקֶט קְצִירְךָ; leket — the fallen stalks or ears left behind during harvesting] you shall not gather [lo telaqqet — לֹא תְלַקֵּט]; for the poor [la-ani — לֶעָנִי; the singular ani — "poor/afflicted one"] and for the sojourner [ve-la-ger — וְלַגֵּר] you shall leave them [ta'azov otam — תַּעֲזֹב אֹתָם]; I am the LORD your God [ani YHWH Eloheikem — אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם].

**23:23** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**23:24** Speak to the sons of Yisra'el, saying: In the seventh month [ba-chodesh ha-shevi'i — בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִי; Tishri, the seventh month by the Nisan-calendar, first month by the ancient autumnal reckoning], on the first of the month [be-echad la-chodesh — בְּאֶחָד לַחֹדֶשׁ], you shall have a rest [yihyeh lakhem shabbaton — יִהְיֶה לָכֶם שַׁבָּתוֹן], a memorial of trumpet blast [zikron teru'ah — זִכְרוֹן תְּרוּעָה; teru'ah — the loud blast; zikron — "memorial/remembrance"; the phrase "memorial of teru'ah" is unique and debated: is it a day of remembrance signaled by trumpet-blowing, or a day on which the teru'ah itself is the memorial-act?], a holy convocation [miqra-qodesh — מִקְרָא-קֹדֶשׁ].

**23:25** All laborious work you shall not do [kol-melechet avodah lo ta'asu — כָּל-מְלֶאכֶת עֲבֹדָה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ], and you shall present a fire-offering to the LORD [ve-hiqravtem isheh la-YHWH — וְהִקְרַבְתֶּם אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה].

**23:26** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**23:27** But on the tenth [akh be-asor — אַךְ בֶּעָשׂוֹר; akh — an adversative particle: "but/however/yet," setting the Day of Atonement in emphatic contrast to what precedes; asor — "the tenth"] of this seventh month [la-chodesh ha-shevi'i ha-zeh — לַחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִי הַזֶּה] is the Day of Atonements [yom ha-kippurim — יוֹם הַכִּפֻּרִים; kippurim — the plural of kippur from k-p-r, "to atone/cover/ransom"; the plural is intensive or comprehensive: "the Day of all-Atonings/the Day of total atonement"]; a holy convocation it shall be for you [miqra-qodesh yihyeh lakhem — מִקְרָא-קֹדֶשׁ יִהְיֶה לָכֶם], and you shall afflict yourselves [ve-innitem et-nafshoteikhem — וְעִנִּיתֶם אֶת-נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם; inui nefesh — "self-affliction of the soul/person"; the Pi'el of '-n-h, "to afflict/humble/oppress"; traditionally understood as fasting and bodily self-denial; the phrase encompasses more than fasting but fasting is the primary expression], and you shall present a fire-offering to the LORD [ve-hiqravtem isheh la-YHWH — וְהִקְרַבְתֶּם אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה].

**23:28** And all labor you shall not do [ve-khol-melakhah lo ta'asu — וְכָל-מְלָאכָה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ] on this very day [be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh — בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה], for it is a Day of Atonements [ki yom kippurim hu — כִּי יוֹם כִּפֻּרִים הוּא], to make atonement for you [le-khapper aleikhem — לְכַפֵּר עֲלֵיכֶם; the Piel of k-p-r — "to atone/cover/ransom"] before the LORD your God [lifnei YHWH Eloheikem — לִפְנֵי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם].

**23:29** For any person [ki kol-ha-nefesh — כִּי כָל-הַנֶּפֶשׁ] who is not afflicted [asher lo-te'unneh — אֲשֶׁר לֹא-תְעֻנֶּה; Pu'al passive — "who is not afflicted/who does not afflict herself"] on this very day [be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh — בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה] shall be cut off [ve-nikhretah — וְנִכְרְתָה; karet — the severest non-capital sanction] from her peoples [me-ammeiha — מֵעַמֶּיהָ; the plural "peoples" refers to the tribal-communal structure; "cut off from her peoples" means severed from the covenant community].

**23:30** And any person [ve-khol-ha-nefesh — וְכָל-הַנֶּפֶשׁ] who does any labor [asher ta'aseh kol-melakhah — אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשֶׂה כָל-מְלָאכָה] on this very day [be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh — בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה] — I will destroy that person [ve-ha'avadti et-ha-nefesh ha-hi — וְהַאֲבַדְתִּי אֶת-הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא; the Hifil of '-b-d, "to cause to perish/to destroy"; first-person divine action] from the midst of her people [mi-qerev ammah — מִקֶּרֶב עַמָּהּ].

**23:31** All labor you shall not do [kol-melakhah lo ta'asu — כָּל-מְלָאכָה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ]; it is a statute forever [chuqqat olam — חֻקַּת עוֹלָם] throughout your generations [le-doroteikem — לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם] in all your dwellings [be-khol moshvoteikhem — בְּכֹל מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם].

**23:32** It is a sabbath of complete rest [shabbat shabbaton hu — שַׁבַּת שַׁבָּתוֹן הוּא] for you [lakhem — לָכֶם], and you shall afflict yourselves [ve-innitem et-nafshoteikhem — וְעִנִּיתֶם אֶת-נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם]; on the ninth of the month in the evening [be-tish'ah la-chodesh ba-erev — בְּתִשְׁעָה לַחֹדֶשׁ בָּעֶרֶב], from evening to evening [me-erev ad-erev — מֵעֶרֶב עַד-עֶרֶב; the Hebrew day runs from sunset to sunset; the Day of Atonement begins at sunset on the 9th and runs until sunset on the 10th], you shall keep [tishbetu — תִּשְׁבְּתוּ; from sh-b-t, "to cease/rest/keep sabbath"] your sabbath [shabba'tekhem — שַׁבַּתְכֶם].

**23:33** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**23:34** Speak to the sons of Yisra'el, saying: On the fifteenth day of this seventh month [ba-chamishah asar yom la-chodesh ha-shevi'i ha-zeh — בַּחֲמִשָּׁה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִי הַזֶּה] is the Feast of Booths [chag ha-sukkot — חַג הַסֻּכּוֹת; sukkot — plural of sukkah, "booth/hut/shelter," a temporary structure of branches; the feast takes its name from the command to dwell in such booths as a memorial of the wilderness] for seven days [shivat yamim — שִׁבְעַת יָמִים] to the LORD.

**23:35** On the first day [ba-yom ha-rishon — בַּיּוֹם הָרִאשׁוֹן] is a holy convocation [miqra-qodesh — מִקְרָא-קֹדֶשׁ]; all laborious work you shall not do [kol-melechet avodah lo ta'asu — כָּל-מְלֶאכֶת עֲבֹדָה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ].

**23:36** For seven days [shivat yamim — שִׁבְעַת יָמִים] you shall present fire-offerings to the LORD [taqrivu isheh la-YHWH — תַּקְרִיבוּ אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה]; on the eighth day [ba-yom ha-shemini — בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי] you shall have a holy convocation [miqra-qodesh yihyeh lakhem — מִקְרָא-קֹדֶשׁ יִהְיֶה לָכֶם] and you shall present a fire-offering to the LORD [ve-hiqravtem isheh la-YHWH — וְהִקְרַבְתֶּם אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה]; it is a solemn assembly [atseret hi — עֲצֶרֶת הִוא; atseret — from '-ts-r, "to restrain/hold back/assemble/close"; a closing assembly; the eighth day is called the atseret — the solemn conclusion — of the feast]; all laborious work you shall not do [kol-melechet avodah lo ta'asu — כָּל-מְלֶאכֶת עֲבֹדָה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ].

**23:37** These are the appointed times of the LORD [elleh mo'adei YHWH — אֵלֶּה מוֹעֲדֵי יְהוָה] which you shall proclaim as holy convocations [asher-tiqre'u otam miqra'ei qodesh — אֲשֶׁר-תִּקְרְאוּ אֹתָם מִקְרָאֵי קֹדֶשׁ] to present a fire-offering to the LORD [le-haqriv isheh la-YHWH — לְהַקְרִיב אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה] — burnt offering and grain offering [olah u-minchah — עֹלָה וּמִנְחָה], sacrifice and drink offerings [zevach u-nesakhim — זֶבַח וּנְסָכִים], each day's matter in its day [devar-yom be-yomo — דְּבַר-יוֹם בְּיוֹמוֹ; lit. "the word of a day in its day" — the daily-appropriate offerings; a phrase denoting the specific requirements belonging to each appointed day].

**23:38** Apart from the sabbaths of the LORD [mi-levad shabbetot YHWH — מִלְּבַד שַׁבְּתוֹת יְהוָה; i.e., the weekly Sabbath] and apart from your gifts [u-mi-levad matnoteikem — וּמִלְּבַד מַתְּנוֹתֵיכֶם; mattanot — "gifts/donations"] and apart from all your vows [u-mi-levad kol-nidreikem — וּמִלְּבַד כָּל-נִדְרֵיכֶם] and apart from all your freewill offerings [u-mi-levad kol-nidvoteikhem — וּמִלְּבַד כָּל-נִדְבֹתֵיכֶם] which you give to the LORD [asher titenu la-YHWH — אֲשֶׁר תִּתְּנוּ לַיהוָה].

**23:39** But on the fifteenth day of the seventh month [akh ba-chamishah asar yom la-chodesh ha-shevi'i — אַךְ בַּחֲמִשָּׁה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִי; akh again marks a fresh emphasis], when you gather in the produce of the land [be-asafkhem et-tevu'at ha-arets — בְּאָסְפְּכֶם אֶת-תְּבוּאַת הָאָרֶץ; tevu'at — "the produce/yield/income of the land"; the agricultural context of harvest-completion], you shall keep [tachoggu — תָּחֹגּוּ; the verb ch-g-g, "to celebrate/keep a pilgrimage feast"; the root of chag — "feast/pilgrimage festival"] the feast of the LORD [et-chag YHWH — אֶת-חַג יְהוָה] for seven days [shivat yamim — שִׁבְעַת יָמִים]; on the first day [ba-yom ha-rishon — בַּיּוֹם הָרִאשׁוֹן] is a rest [shabbaton — שַׁבָּתוֹן] and on the eighth day [u-va-yom ha-shemini — וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי] is a rest [shabbaton — שַׁבָּתוֹן].

**23:40** And you shall take for yourselves [u-lqachtem lakhem — וּלְקַחְתֶּם לָכֶם] on the first day [ba-yom ha-rishon — בַּיּוֹם הָרִאשׁוֹן] the fruit of a majestic tree [peri ets hadar — פְּרִי עֵץ הָדָר; ets hadar — "a majestic/splendid tree"; hadar — "splendor/majesty/glory"; the specific species is not named in the text; later identified by tradition as the etrog/citron], fronds of palm trees [kappot temarim — כַּפֹּת תְּמָרִים; kappot — "palm branches/fronds"; temarim — "date palms"], and boughs of leafy trees [ve-anaf ets avot — וַעֲנַף עֵץ-עָבֹת; anaf — "branch"; avot — "thick/interwoven/dense"; the leafy tree whose branches intertwine; later identified as myrtle], and willows of the brook [ve-arvei-nachal — וְעַרְבֵי-נָחַל; arvei — plural of aravah, "willow/poplar"; nachal — "wadi/stream-bed"; willows growing by watercourses], and you shall rejoice before the LORD your God [u-semachtem lifnei YHWH Eloheikem — וּשְׂמַחְתֶּם לִפְנֵי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם] for seven days [shivat yamim — שִׁבְעַת יָמִים].

**23:41** And you shall keep it [ve-chaggotem oto — וְחַגֹּתֶם אֹתוֹ] as a feast to the LORD [chag la-YHWH — חַג לַיהוָה] seven days in the year [shivat yamim ba-shanah — שִׁבְעַת יָמִים בַּשָּׁנָה]; it is a statute forever [chuqqat olam — חֻקַּת עוֹלָם] throughout your generations [le-doroteikem — לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם]; in the seventh month [ba-chodesh ha-shevi'i — בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִי] you shall keep it [tachoggu oto — תָּחֹגּוּ אֹתוֹ].

**23:42** In booths you shall dwell [ba-sukkot teshvu — בַּסֻּכֹּת תֵּשְׁבוּ; the foundational commandment of the feast, stated as a bare imperative: "in booths — you shall dwell"; the fronted ba-sukkot emphasizes the booths themselves as the point] seven days [shivat yamim — שִׁבְעַת יָמִים]; every native [kol-ha-ezrach — כָּל-הָאֶזְרָח; ezrach — "a native/one born in the land," the native Israelite as distinct from the sojourner] in Yisra'el [be-Yisra'el — בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל] shall dwell in booths [yeshvu ba-sukkot — יֵשְׁבוּ בַּסֻּכֹּת].

**23:43** So that your generations may know [le-ma'an yede'u doroteikem — לְמַעַן יֵדְעוּ דֹרֹתֵיכֶם] that in booths I caused the sons of Yisra'el to dwell [ki ba-sukkot hoshavti et-benei-Yisra'el — כִּי בַסֻּכּוֹת הוֹשַׁבְתִּי אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל; the Hifil hoshavti — "I caused to dwell/I made to settle"; the LORD as the active agent of Israel's wilderness sojourn] when I brought them out of the land of Egypt [be-hotsi'i otam me-erets Mitsrayim — בְּהוֹצִיאִי אוֹתָם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם]; I am the LORD your God [ani YHWH Eloheikem — אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם].

**23:44** And Moshe declared [va-yedabber Moshe — וַיְדַבֵּר מֹשֶׁה; the closing summary verse shifts to narrative past tense, confirming transmission] the appointed times of the LORD [et-mo'adei YHWH — אֶת-מוֹעֲדֵי יְהוָה] to the sons of Yisra'el [el-benei-Yisra'el — אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Overview

Leviticus 23 is the sacred-calendar chapter of the Holiness Code, and one of the most structurally complex passages in the Torah. Its governing concept is the mo'ed (plural: mo'adim) — the "appointed time/meeting." The word is built on the root y-'-d, "to appoint/fix a time," closely related to mo'ed as "tent of meeting" (ohel mo'ed). The mo'adim are not merely annual festivals but divinely appointed intervals of encounter between the LORD and his people: fixed coordinates of sacred time analogous to the sanctuary as a fixed coordinate of sacred space. The chapter announces in v.2 that these times are "my appointed times" (mo'adai) — they belong to the LORD, and Israel's role is to "proclaim" them (tiqre'u, from q-r-'), not merely to observe them. The act of proclamation (miqra) itself constitutes the holy convocation.

The calendar established in chapter 23 covers a full liturgical year: the weekly Sabbath (the temporal foundation of all sacred time), two spring festivals (Passover/Unleavened Bread and Shavuot/Weeks, with the omer-count binding them together), and three autumn festivals in the seventh month (Trumpets, Day of Atonement, and Tabernacles/Sukkot). The seventh month is distinctively dense: three appointed times in a single month, with the climactic Day of Atonement as the theological center of the sacred year. The chapter's seven-fold structure mirrors the Sabbath-pattern: six working months frame one month of concentrated sacred time.

Four recurring structural elements mark each appointed time: (1) the temporal specification (date and month); (2) miqra-qodesh (holy convocation); (3) prohibition of melaket avodah (laborious work); and (4) fire-offerings (isheh) to the LORD. The Day of Atonement adds a fifth element — inui nefesh (self-affliction) — and the Sabbath and Day of Atonement are uniquely designated shabbat shabbaton ("a sabbath of complete rest"), the most intense rest-formula in the Torah.

### Verse-by-Verse Notes

**v.2 — mo'adei YHWH / miqra'ei qodesh:** The double description "appointed times of the LORD / holy convocations" establishes two dimensions of each festival: the temporal (a fixed time in the calendar year, divinely appointed) and the cultic-communal (a gathering called by proclamation). The root of miqra (q-r-', "to call/proclaim") is the same as the root of miqra in a later technical sense ("scripture/reading"), but here it is purely functional: the public proclamation that brings the community into convocation. Israel does not merely observe the mo'adim passively; they actively call them into being by proclamation.

**v.3 — shabbat shabbaton / be-khol moshvoteikhem:** The weekly Sabbath appears first in the festival calendar not as an introduction to the festivals but as their temporal foundation. The festivals are set within a week-structured time, and the week itself is constituted by the Sabbath. The phrase be-khol moshvoteikhem ("in all your dwellings") is crucial: the Sabbath is not a sanctuary-based observance but a universal one applying wherever Israel lives. This is the same phrase that appears in vv.14, 17, 21, 31 for the other festivals — suggesting that the mo'adim as a whole are to be observed in every community, not only at the central sanctuary.

**v.3 — shabbat hi la-YHWH:** "It is a sabbath to the LORD" — the Sabbath belongs to the LORD (la-YHWH), as do all the mo'adim (mo'adei YHWH, v.2). The Sabbath is not Israel's creation or possession; it is the LORD's. Israel's role is custodianship and observance within a sacred time-structure that pre-exists the covenant (cf. Gen 2:2–3, where the seventh-day rest is built into creation before human history begins).

**v.5 — bein ha-arbayim:** "Between the two evenings" — the expression describes the period of transition between afternoon and night. It has been variously understood as: (a) between 3pm and sunset (the Pharisaic/rabbinic interpretation), or (b) between sunset and full dark (the Samaritan and Karaite interpretation). The Pesach sacrifice occurs in this liminal time — neither day nor night — appropriate for the night on which Israel left Egypt "in haste" (Exod 12:11). The text does not specify the precise hour; it locates the sacrifice at the boundary between day and night, which is itself symbolically appropriate for the transition-event of the Exodus.

**v.6 — chag ha-matsot / matsot tokhelu:** The Feast of Unleavened Bread begins the day after Passover (15th Nisan) and lasts seven days. The command matsot tokhelu ("you shall eat unleavened bread") is stated simply, without the detailed hamets-prohibition of Exod 12 and 13; this chapter presupposes familiarity with those earlier texts. The word matsot (unleavened bread) is the bread of haste (Exod 12:39 — "for it was not leavened because they were driven out of Egypt and could not delay"). The festival memorializes the Exodus through the daily consumption of the same bread that marked the departure.

**v.7 — melechet avodah:** The compound melechet avodah (lit. "labor-work/service-work") is stronger than simple melakhah ("work"). The noun avodah can mean "service/slavery/labor" — the same word used for Israel's slavery in Egypt (avodat mitsrayim). The prohibition of melechet avodah on festival days thus has an implicit Exodus-resonance: the day on which Israel was freed from Egypt's avodah is a day on which all avodah-labor ceases. The distinction between melakhah (ordinary work, totally prohibited on the Sabbath and Day of Atonement) and melechet avodah (occupational/laborious work, prohibited on the other festival days) is a significant legal-halakhic distinction elaborated in rabbinic literature.

**v.11 — mi-macharat ha-shabbat:** "On the day after the sabbath" — this phrase is the center of a long-standing calendrical controversy between Pharisees (later rabbinic Judaism), who understood "the sabbath" as the first day of Unleavened Bread (15th Nisan, the first festival rest-day), and Sadducees, Boethusians, Samaritans, and Karaites, who understood it as the weekly Sabbath that falls during Unleavened Bread. The former interpretation fixes the omer-waving and the counting to Shavuot on a fixed calendar date (16th Nisan), producing a fixed date for Shavuot (6th Sivan). The latter interpretation makes the omer-waving fall on the Sunday during Unleavened Bread, so Shavuot always falls on a Sunday. The EMET translation preserves the Hebrew literally: mi-macharat ha-shabbat — "from the day after the sabbath." The text is genuinely ambiguous on which "sabbath" is meant; a literal translation does not resolve what the Hebrew does not resolve.

**v.14 — etsem ha-yom ha-zeh:** "This very day" — the expression etsem ha-yom (lit. "the bone/substance of the day") is an intensifying idiom in Hebrew. It appears three times in this chapter (vv.14, 21, 28, 30) and elsewhere in the Torah (notably Exod 12:17, 51; Deut 32:48). The sense is "the very day itself" — the specific, irreducible day, not merely "that day." Its use for both the omer-day (v.14) and the Day of Atonement (vv.28, 30) reinforces the precision and gravity of each observance: no substitution or shifting of the day is permissible.

**v.15 — u-sefartem lakhem / sheva shabbatot temimot:** "And you shall count for yourselves" — the command sefirat ha-omer (the counting of the omer) is here established as a personal, household obligation: lakhem — "for yourselves." Each person counts, not only the priest or community leader. The count is of sheva shabbatot temimot — "seven complete weeks" — where shabbatot means "weeks" (the seven-day cycle) and temimot ("complete/whole/without defect") insists on the integrity of the count: no days may be skipped or added. The word temimot connects back to tamim in the sacrificial-animal laws and to the priestly wholeness laws of chapter 21 — wholeness/completeness is demanded of body, sacrifice, and time alike.

**v.17 — chamets te'afenah / bikkurim:** "They shall be baked leavened" — uniquely, the two loaves of Shavuot (lechem ha-bikkurim — the firstfruits bread) are to be made with chamets (leaven), in stark contrast to the unleavened bread of Passover and the prohibition of leaven in altar-offerings (Lev 2:11). The leavened character of the Shavuot loaves represents the new-grain offering as it is actually consumed in daily life — ordinary, fully-leavened bread — brought as bikkurim (firstfruits) from the grain harvest. The two loaves (shnayim — two) are unusual; the dual presentation may represent the two components of the grain harvest (barley and wheat) or may have a structural parallel with the two goats of Yom Kippur or the two lambs of the daily tamid. The bikkurim label connects Shavuot to the bikkurim-offering regulated in Deut 26:1–11.

**v.24 — zikron teru'ah / yom teru'ah:** "A memorial of trumpet blast" — this day (1st Tishri) is called here zikron teru'ah, a "memorial/remembrance of teru'ah." Numbers 29:1 calls it yom teru'ah — "a day of trumpet blast." The teru'ah is a distinctive type of sound — a staccato, broken blast — as opposed to the teki'ah (a straight sustained blast). In later Jewish tradition, the shofar (ram's horn) is the instrument of teru'ah. The word zikron ("memorial/remembrance") is theologically significant: the teru'ah does not merely mark the day; it is a memorial-act, recalling something. The tradition connects the teru'ah of the New Year to the binding of Isaac (Akedah), but the text itself gives no explicit referent for the zikron. The day's full designation in later tradition is Rosh Hashanah ("Head/Beginning of the Year"), though the text calls it the first day of the seventh month — 7th month by the sacred calendar that begins in Nisan, but the first month of the agricultural/civil year.

**v.27 — akh be-asor / yom ha-kippurim / inui nefesh:** The particle akh ("but/however") introduces the Day of Atonement with a solemn contrast. The plural kippurim ("atonings") has been variously interpreted: as a plural of intensity (the comprehensive, all-encompassing atonement), a plural of abstraction, or a plural denoting multiple acts of atonement (the seven-fold sprinkling of the Lev 16 ceremony). All three interpretations have textual support; the EMET translation preserves the plural "Day of Atonements." The term inui nefesh ("self-affliction of the soul/person") covers the range of bodily self-denial: fasting (most prominently), abstaining from bathing, anointing, wearing leather sandals, and marital relations (the five abstentions enumerated in Mishnah Yoma 8:1). The text specifies only inui nefesh, leaving the specific forms of denial to elaboration by oral tradition and later halakhah.

**v.28 — le-khapper aleikhem lifnei YHWH Eloheikem:** "To make atonement for you before the LORD your God" — the purpose clause for the Day of Atonement's labor-prohibition is remarkable: one does not cease labor on Yom Kippur because it is a sabbath (though it is called shabbat shabbaton) but because it is a day of atonement — le-khapper. The kipper-process is the work of the Day; all other work must cease so that the atonement-work can be done. The phrase "before the LORD your God" (lifnei YHWH Eloheikem) echoes the cultic geography of Lev 16 where the high priest enters before the divine presence in the Holy of Holies. The community's inui nefesh and cessation of labor corresponds to the priest's entry before the veil: both are oriented toward the single goal of atonement in the divine presence.

**v.29-30 — karet vs. divine destruction:** Verses 29 and 30 describe two different consequences for failure to observe Yom Kippur. Verse 29 (failure to afflict oneself) results in karet — "she shall be cut off from her peoples." Verse 30 (performing labor on Yom Kippur) results in divine first-person destruction: "I will destroy that person from the midst of her people" (ve-ha'avadti). The distinction is significant: karet is a sanction named in legal terms (the person is "cut off"), while the v.30 formula is a direct divine action (va-ha'avadti — "I will cause to perish"). The progressive severity of the two formulas reflects the seriousness of the labor-prohibition: actively working on Yom Kippur is an offense not merely against the community (karet) but against the LORD directly (divine destruction).

**v.32 — me-erev ad-erev tishbetu shabba'tekhem:** "From evening to evening you shall keep your sabbath" — uniquely, Yom Kippur specifies the temporal boundary of its observance: from sunset on the 9th to sunset on the 10th. This is the only mo'ed for which the evening-to-evening duration is explicitly stated in Leviticus 23 (though the principle applies implicitly to all holy days in which labor is prohibited). The specification be-tish'ah la-chodesh ba-erev ("on the ninth of the month in the evening") means the observance begins not on the 10th at sunrise but on the 9th at sundown — a full 25-hour day by later reckoning.

**v.36 — atseret hi:** "It is a solemn assembly/closing assembly" — the atseret (from '-ts-r, "to restrain/hold/detain") is the closing convocation of Sukkot. The word implies a gathering in which the participants are "held/detained" at the sacred site — a closing day that holds the congregation together at the end of the pilgrimage. The eighth day of Sukkot (Shemini Atseret — "the Eighth Day of Closing Assembly") becomes in later tradition a festival of its own, distinct from the seven days of Sukkot proper. In the Diaspora, a ninth day was added (Simchat Torah — "Joy of the Torah"). The text presents it as the culminating day of the seven-day feast: after seven days of dwelling in booths, the eighth day is a holy convocation with fire-offerings but without the booth-dwelling requirement (which applies only "seven days," v.42).

**v.37 — devar-yom be-yomo:** "The matter of a day in its day" — this phrase, unique in the Torah, means that each appointed day has its own specific sacrificial requirements. The principle devar-yom be-yomo ("the word/matter of a day in its day") is an organizing principle for the elaborate sacrifice-lists of Numbers 28–29, which specify the exact offerings for each mo'ed. The mention of four categories — olah (burnt offering), minchah (grain offering), zevach (sacrifice/peace offering), and nesakhim (drink offerings) — covers the full range of altar-presentations possible on a festival day.

**v.40 — peri ets hadar / kappot temarim / anaf ets avot / arvei nachal:** The four species (arba minim) of verse 40 are the foundation of the Sukkot liturgical practice of taking and waving the lulav. The identification of each species by later tradition: (1) peri ets hadar = etrog (citron); (2) kappot temarim = lulav (date-palm frond); (3) anaf ets avot = hadassim (myrtle branches); (4) arvei nachal = aravot (willow branches). The text itself does not name these species; it uses descriptive terms only. The hadar ("majestic/glorious") quality of the first tree suggests aesthetic excellence; the interwoven/thick branches (avot) of the third suggest the dense leafy quality associated with myrtle. The command ve-semachtem lifnei YHWH ("you shall rejoice before the LORD") is the only festival-law in Leviticus that explicitly commands joy (simchah). Deuteronomy 16:14–15 elaborates the joy of Sukkot as the characteristic emotion of the feast.

**v.42 — ba-sukkot teshvu / kol-ha-ezrach be-Yisra'el:** "In booths you shall dwell — every native in Yisra'el" — the fronting of ba-sukkot (in booths) before the verb teshvu (you shall dwell) is a Hebrew emphasis-structure: "In booths — that is where you shall dwell." The ezrach (native-born Israelite) is the subject; the text does not mention the ger (sojourner) here, in contrast to the Passover and other laws that extend observance to the ger. The silence may be significant: booth-dwelling as a memorial of the wilderness sojourn is specifically an Israelite memory — it recalls the experience of the generation that the LORD brought out of Egypt, not the experience of sojourners who joined later. However, Num 15:15–16 establishes a general principle of equal obligation for sojourner and native; the legal silence in v.42 does not necessarily exclude the ger.

**v.43 — ki ba-sukkot hoshavti et-benei-Yisra'el:** The theological grounding of Sukkot: "for in booths I caused the sons of Yisra'el to dwell when I brought them out of the land of Egypt." The Hifil hoshavti (from y-sh-v, "to sit/dwell" — causative: "I caused to dwell") makes the LORD the active agent of Israel's wilderness sojourn. The sukkot of the wilderness were not Israel's achievement or resourcefulness; the LORD himself "caused them to dwell" in temporary shelters. This reframes the annual booth-dwelling from nostalgia or agriculture into theology: to dwell in a booth during Sukkot is to re-enact the LORD's sovereign provision and the people's dependent trust during the wilderness years. The Exodus-grounding formula that closes the chapter (ani YHWH Eloheikem) recapitulates the chapter's foundation: the sacred calendar of the LORD's appointed times is the calendar of a redeemed people, structured by the memory of the Exodus and the anticipation of the land.

**v.44 — va-yedabber Moshe:** The chapter closes with a brief narrative summary verse: Moshe "declared" (va-yedabber — Piel of d-b-r, "to speak/declare") the mo'adim to the sons of Yisra'el. This closing formula confirms that the entire chapter is Moshe's speech to Israel, authorized by the LORD's speech to Moshe (vv.1–2). The chapter thus ends not with a law but with an act of transmission: Moshe faithfully declares what he has received. This pattern (divine speech → Moshe → Israel) is the transmission-formula of the Holiness Code and of the Torah as a whole.

---

## Key Term Table

| Hebrew | Transliteration | EMET Rendering | Notes |
|--------|----------------|----------------|-------|
| מוֹעֲדֵי יְהוָה | mo'adei YHWH | "the appointed times of the LORD" | From y-'-d, "to appoint/meet"; the fixed sacred times of encounter |
| מִקְרָא-קֹדֶשׁ | miqra-qodesh | "holy convocation" | From q-r-', "to call/proclaim"; a summoned holy assembly |
| שַׁבַּת שַׁבָּתוֹן | shabbat shabbaton | "a sabbath of complete rest" | Intensive construction; the most intensive rest-formula; used for weekly Sabbath and Yom Kippur |
| מְלֶאכֶת עֲבֹדָה | melechet avodah | "laborious work" | Stronger than ordinary melakhah; "service-labor"; prohibited on festival days |
| פֶּסַח | pesach | "Passover" | From p-s-ch, "to pass over/skip over"; name of the sacrifice and festival |
| בֵּין הָעַרְבָּיִם | bein ha-arbayim | "between the two evenings" | The liminal transitional period between afternoon and nightfall |
| חַג הַמַּצּוֹת | chag ha-matsot | "the Feast of Unleavened Bread" | Seven days of matsot, beginning 15th Nisan |
| עֹמֶר | omer | "sheaf/omer" | The first-grain sheaf waved before the LORD; also a unit of measure |
| מִמָּחֳרַת הַשַּׁבָּת | mi-macharat ha-shabbat | "on the day after the sabbath" | The disputed phrase determining the date of Shavuot; "sabbath" = ?which sabbath? |
| שֶׁבַע שַׁבָּתוֹת תְּמִימֹת | sheva shabbatot temimot | "seven complete weeks" | The omer-counting period; temimot = "complete/whole/without defect" |
| בִּכּוּרִים | bikkurim | "firstfruits" | The first-fruits offering; two leavened loaves presented on Shavuot |
| חָמֵץ | chamets | "leavened" | The one case where leavened bread is commanded as a sanctuary offering |
| זִכְרוֹן תְּרוּעָה | zikron teru'ah | "memorial of trumpet blast" | The descriptor for 1st Tishri; teru'ah = staccato blast |
| יוֹם הַכִּפֻּרִים | yom ha-kippurim | "the Day of Atonements" | Plural kippurim = comprehensive/intensive atonement |
| עִנּוּי נֶפֶשׁ | inui nefesh | "self-affliction of the soul" | Pi'el of '-n-h; fasting and bodily self-denial; covers all forms of Yom Kippur self-denial |
| עֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה | etsem ha-yom ha-zeh | "this very day" | Intensifying idiom; "the bone/substance of this day" |
| כָּרֵת | karet | "cutting off" | Severest non-capital sanction; excision from the covenant community |
| חַג הַסֻּכּוֹת | chag ha-sukkot | "the Feast of Booths" | Sukkot = plural of sukkah, "booth/temporary shelter" |
| עֲצֶרֶת | atseret | "solemn assembly" | From '-ts-r, "to restrain/hold"; the closing assembly of Sukkot (8th day) |
| פְּרִי עֵץ הָדָר | peri ets hadar | "fruit of a majestic tree" | Later identified as etrog/citron; hadar = "splendor/majesty" |
| כַּפֹּת תְּמָרִים | kappot temarim | "fronds of palm trees" | The lulav; palm-fronds |
| עֲנַף עֵץ עָבֹת | anaf ets avot | "boughs of leafy trees" | Later identified as myrtle; avot = "thick/interwoven" |
| עַרְבֵי-נָחַל | arvei-nachal | "willows of the brook" | Willows growing by stream-beds |
| בַּסֻּכּוֹת תֵּשְׁבוּ | ba-sukkot teshvu | "in booths you shall dwell" | The foundational commandment of Sukkot; fronted ba-sukkot = emphasis on the booths |
| אֶזְרָח | ezrach | "native" | Native-born Israelite; contrasted with ger (sojourner) |
| דְּבַר-יוֹם בְּיוֹמוֹ | devar-yom be-yomo | "the matter of a day in its day" | Each festival day has its specific, day-appropriate offerings |
| תְּרוּמָה | terumah | "contribution" | The elevated offering-portion; here in contexts of Shavuot grain-offering |
| חֻקַּת עוֹלָם | chuqqat olam | "a statute forever" | The permanent-binding formula; appears four times in this chapter |

## LXX Variants (Codex Vaticanus, pp. 109–110)

**v.2 — mo'adei YHWH / miqra'ei qodesh:** LXX *hai heortai kyriou / klētai hagiai* — "the feasts of the Lord / holy callings." The LXX renders mo'adim as heortai ("feasts"), losing the "appointed meeting" nuance of mo'ed and substituting the generic Greek festival-word. The miqra'ei qodesh ("holy convocations/callings") is rendered klētai hagiai ("holy summonings/callings"), which accurately captures the q-r-' root but sounds more abstract than the Hebrew. Vaticanus is consistent in using heortai for mo'adim throughout chapter 23.

**v.3 — shabbat shabbaton:** LXX *sabbata anapausis* — "a sabbath of rest." The Greek anapausis ("rest/repose") renders shabbaton; the LXX lacks the intensive doubling that shabbaton carries as a cognate-intensive noun in Hebrew. The phrase does not convey "sabbath of sabbathing" or "rest of complete rest" as strongly as the MT.

**v.5 — bein ha-arbayim:** LXX *pros hesperas* — "toward evening." The LXX collapses bein ha-arbayim ("between the two evenings") to a simple temporal marker "toward evening," losing the precise twilight-period nuance of the MT. This rendering supports the simpler interpretation (late afternoon/evening) but eliminates the ambiguity about exactly when the sacrifice was to be performed.

**v.11 — mi-macharat ha-shabbat:** LXX *tē epaurion tēs prōtēs* — "on the day after the first [day]." The LXX here renders ha-shabbat as "the first [day]" (tēs prōtēs) — apparently understanding the "sabbath" of the omer-count as the first day of Unleavened Bread, supporting the Pharisaic/rabbinic interpretation that the omer is waved on 16th Nisan. This is an interpretive translation: the LXX resolves the ambiguity of mi-macharat ha-shabbat by identifying which sabbath is meant. The Vaticanus reading at this verse is among the most significant LXX variants in Leviticus for its calendrical implications.

**v.14 — ve-karmel:** LXX *chidra* — a transliteration (from Hebrew) rather than a translation. The LXX apparently did not have a Greek equivalent for karmel (fresh full grain ears) and so retained the Hebrew word in transliterated form. This is one of the relatively rare instances in the Pentateuch LXX where a Hebrew term is transliterated rather than translated, reflecting the translator's uncertainty about the term's precise referent.

**v.15 — sheva shabbatot temimot:** LXX *hepta hebdomadas holoklerōs* — "seven weeks completely/wholly." The LXX renders temimot as holoklerōs ("wholly/completely"), an adverb qualifying the counting rather than an adjective modifying the weeks. This is a reasonable rendering of temimot but loses the sacrificial-purity resonance of tamim/temimot as applied here to the weeks themselves.

**v.17 — chamets te'afenah:** LXX *arton semligan artion ex artemitōn* — this Vaticanus reading involves some textual difficulty. The basic sense — that the loaves are to be leavened — is preserved: the LXX uses artion (leavened) in contrast to the Passover matsot. The distinctive command to bake leavened offerings (normally forbidden on the altar) is thus retained in the LXX.

**v.22 — pe'at sadkha / leket qetsirekha:** LXX *akran tou agrou sou / drygmata tou amētou sou* — "the corner of your field / gleanings of your harvest." The LXX renders pe'ah as akra ("corner/edge") and leket as drygmata ("things dragged together/gleanings"). The rendering is accurate but somewhat more technical than the Hebrew's simple leket (from l-q-t, "to glean/pick up"). The social-charity laws embedded within the Shavuot section (v.22) are fully preserved in the LXX.

**v.24 — zikron teru'ah:** LXX *mnēmosunon salpismatōn* — "a memorial of trumpet blasts." The LXX renders teru'ah as salpismatōn (from salpizō — "to sound a trumpet"), specifically translating the teru'ah as a trumpet sound. The word mnēmosunon ("memorial/remembrance") accurately renders zikron. The combination mnēmosunon salpismatōn is the LXX's contribution to the later identification of this day with trumpet-blowing; the NT's use of salpinx in eschatological contexts (e.g., 1 Thess 4:16; Rev 8–9) may distantly echo this usage.

**v.27 — yom ha-kippurim:** LXX *hēmera exilasmou* — "the day of propitiation/expiation." The Greek exilasmos (from exilaskomai — "to propitiate/make favorable/expiate") renders kippurim. The choice of exilasmos rather than hilasmos is significant: exilasmos emphasizes the removal of defilement and propitiation of divine anger, while the MT's kipper focuses on covering/ransoming. Paul's use of hilastērion in Rom 3:25 (and the LXX's use of hilastērion for the kaporet/mercy seat in Exod 25:17–21) draws on related vocabulary. The Day of Atonement terminology in Greek has been foundational for Pauline and Hebrews Christology.

**v.27 — ve-innitem et-nafshoteikhem:** LXX *tapeinōsete tas psychas hymōn* — "you shall humble your souls/selves." The LXX renders the Piel of '-n-h ("to afflict/oppress/humble") as tapeinōsete ("to humble/abase"). This is a theologically freighted choice: tapeinos/tapeinōsis ("humility/lowliness") is a virtue-term in LXX and later Greek-Jewish and Christian literature, while the Hebrew root '-n-h in the Piel primarily denotes affliction and deprivation. The LXX subtly reframes inui nefesh as an act of humble self-abasement rather than primarily one of physical self-denial.

**v.32 — me-erev ad-erev:** LXX *ap' hesperas heōs hesperas* — "from evening to evening." The LXX renders the temporal formula accurately, preserving the evening-to-evening duration of Yom Kippur. This is one of the explicit statements in the Torah that confirms the Hebrew day runs from sunset to sunset, reflected identically in the LXX.

**v.36 — atseret hi:** LXX *exodos estai hymin* — "it shall be a departure/sending-out for you." The LXX renders atseret as exodos, the Greek word for "departure" — the same word used for the title of the second book of the Torah. This is a bold interpretive rendering: the LXX understands atseret not as "assembly" but as "departure/sending-out" (the closing of the feast, the dismissal of the congregation). The choice of exodos connects the closing of Sukkot with the Exodus-theme that runs through the chapter — the atseret as a "departure" echoing the great Exodus. This rendering is unique to the LXX and is not followed by other ancient versions (Vulgate: conclusio; Peshitta preserves the term).

**v.40 — peri ets hadar:** LXX *karpon xylou horaion* — "fruit of a beautiful tree." The LXX renders hadar ("majestic/glorious") as horaion ("beautiful/lovely/seasonable"), a common Greek aesthetic term. The translation captures the visual attractiveness of the fruit but loses the royal-majestic resonance of hadar in Hebrew (used of divine glory in many Psalms: e.g., Ps 8:1, 104:1). The four-species command is rendered in the LXX without identification of the species, as in the MT.

**v.43 — ki ba-sukkot hoshavti et-benei-Yisra'el:** LXX *hoti en skenais katōkisa tous hyious Israēl* — "for in tents I caused the sons of Israel to dwell." The LXX renders sukkot as skenais ("tents"), the same word used for the tabernacle (skēnē). This is theologically resonant: the people dwelt in "tents" (sukkot) during the wilderness, and the LORD's presence dwelt in the Tent (ohel/skēnē). The memorial of Sukkot is thus connected in the LXX to the tabernacle-theology of the Exodus narratives: Israel in their sukkot and the LORD in his skēnē — the same root, the same wilderness sojourn, the same God-with-people dynamic.

---

*EMET translation completed. Leviticus 23:1–44. All 44 verses rendered. Source: Leningrad Codex folios 71v–72r. LXX: Vaticanus pp. 109–110.*
